So small businesses from time to time do have startup costs, but a lot of times your average small business is coming into it with like, hey, we're going to sell e-commerce, right?

So with the partnerships, right, they end up paying themselves as an employee, right?
And so a partner cannot be an employee.
I mean, we see it a lot.
And so no one told them that, right?
So that's like crucial information for them to know, hey, you can't pay yourself as an employee if you're in a partnership, right?
So that's probably a really big one.

So single LLC.
So

I'm going to do this S election.

The mileage, all of the things that they are used to being able to claim, they can't get that anymore without a selection, right?
And so one of the things that most, if not, I won't say all, because I'm sure there are a few out there, but most S corp owners are not familiar with the term accountable plan.
And so that's something that they could set up through payroll.
that's with that.

So to go back to the partnership thing you mentioned, so let's say someone who's in a partnership, like has been paying themselves as an employee, what are like the consequences of that and how can they like walk that back or like try to fix it?

So I would say, you know, luckily, I should probably knock on wood, I have not seen any like true consequences, like from an IRS standpoint, you know, a lot of times a client will have taken a small amount in payroll.
So that payroll taxes...
It's been sent right to the government.
It's pretty hard to get it back.
So it's one of those things.
It's like, okay, let's correct it as soon as we identify the problem.
Because what happens is you run the risk of the IRS coming back and saying, hey, you weren't supposed to pay yourself on payroll.
And then, you know, again, it kind of depends on how the IRS wants to handle it.
But you may have to amend payroll forms, try and get the money back on taxes because in a partnership setting, right, it's guaranteed payments.
That's how you're paid.
But it's subject to self-employment tax on the partner, right?
And so of no amount of that tax is an expense to the partnership.
But when you run it through payroll, the partnership ends up paying the tax and expensing it.
So, you know, it's best to correct it.
As soon as it's figured out, because if the IRS kind of looked into it, they may say, hey, we're going to disallow this payroll tax expense, right?
And now you've got to pay it on your individual return, but then good luck getting that refund back, right, for the taxes that were paid.
Okay, so we can get pretty quickly, very quickly.
Yeah, I get it.

But in terms of just, I know like this is very different probably from one industry to the next, but just talking kind of broadly, are there any really like expensive mistakes that like small, medium-sized businesses make when it comes to taxes that are really avoidable if you work with like the right CPA or town?
Yeah, so I would say the biggest, the biggest like thing to avoid any kind of like surprises.
It doesn't.
At the end of the day is to do tax planning and to have regular check-ins, so quarterly check-ins.

One of the things that becomes quite costly on a partnership entity or an S-corp entity is they will take a ton of distributions, and rightfully so, because it's their money, they're earning it, and they're going to be taxed on it.

But so the common misconception is I'm going to take all these distributions, and so they're taking distributions in excess of basis, and then what ends up happening is now they're paying tax on their distributions, and so it's kind of a universal rule, distributions aren't taxable, and they aren't until they exceed the shareholder or the partner's basis.
And so that's a common one that we see, and so kind of checking in along the way through.

About the year, doing the quarterly check-in, seeing where the profit is at, and saying, you know, kind of looking at the distributions they're taking and saying, hey, like maybe this quarter don't take that distribution, or you're getting to the end of the year, maybe you need to put $20,000 back into the company to avoid that taxation, because they're already paying the tax on the income already.
So it's like, you know, whatever their affected tax rate is, let's say, you know, for federal purposes, let's say it's 25%.
They've already paid 25% on the income.
Now the distribution on excess basis is going to come in at like 15% long-term profitable gain rates.
And so definitely want to avoid that at all costs.
Okay, that makes sense.
In terms of revenue growth, let's say you have a business that makes...
They $500,000 in one year, and the next year, they make $2 million in revenue.
How does that impact their tax planning strategy and how they work with their CPA?
Yeah, so I would say, and it kind of does depend on the entity structure, right?
So from a C-Corp standpoint, the company is going to pay the tax, and then the partnership and the S-Corp, the individual will pay the tax.
So that's a significant step up in income.
And so, again, that's one of those things where you have to heavily rely on your CPA for that tax planning because, you know, if you have that $500,000, maybe you're paying estimates, right?
So the corporation could be paying estimates or the individual for the partnership and S-Corp can be paying those estimates, but now they've quadrupled, right?
Right, so now it's...
it's...
Like, okay, hey, let's check in.
Let's adjust these estimates so there's not this huge surprise at the end of the year.
Or, you know, some clients haven't paid estimates, right?
And they may have some losses.
And so tax planning is, like, so crucial for all of our small businesses because they may have losses, right, that they're continuing to carry forward.
And it's like, okay, well, maybe it's $2 million in revenue this year, but we can utilize, you know, a million of that loss.
And so now we're kind of looking at this.
But it also gives us time, too, to look at, like, what can we leverage here for a lower tax liability?
And so it could be something as simple as, hey, we've been meaning to need a new vehicle, right?
And it's like, well, you know, we can go ahead and suggest that you do this.
This specific credit for this vehicle, right, is expiring at the end of 2020.
25, or at the end of 2026, so we can get you the credit and we can get you bonus depreciation.

So there becomes an issue with a step up in basis, right?
And so that becomes important because when you go to sell the property later on, you know, the difference between your basis and what you sell it for becomes a taxable game.
So that's kind of one of the issues, right, with putting it into a C corp structure or an S.
But one of the biggest issues is, let's say you want to acquire another property, right?

The partnership itself is going to take out this $2 million mortgage and we're going to take on another property and rent it.

So we can, I can take a million and you can take a million into our basis in the partnership.
Under the S-Corp and the C-Corp, that's not a thing.
So, you know, that kind of helps when you are, in the case of distributions, right?

Yeah.
So I think one of, I mean, it's all over the place in terms of like what we see distribution wise.
One of the things is even if it's just a, like a single person, right, who is taking on a Schedule E on their individual return, but they own the property with someone else.
And so they're taking half.
A lot of times what happens is they don't know how to take their half of the basis of the property and depreciate it.
But another thing is the actual establishing of the basis of the property is kind of a common issue.
And when I just recently worked through, they said, well, you know, the asking price was $450,000, right, for the property.
So we paid $450,000 and I said, well, hey, send me the closing statement.
And they did.

And so that's still part of the basis.
That's still, you know, a chunk of money that had to come out to purchase this property that should be depreciated.
But also when you fast forward to if they do a 1031 exchange or they just.
Oh, the property.
They're missing that nice little chunk right there of, you know, basis where now they're paying this taxable gain and that would help against that taxable gain.
So the building the basis is probably the biggest thing.
And then for rental real estate specifically, people will do improvements and they think, oh, because I've done this improvement.
It's, it's literally just that, or it's a repair.
And there are a lot of credits.
A lot are getting ready to probably expire since the bill passed.

And so we ended up depreciating this $40,000 expense over 27 and a half years.
There could be credits there for them.
Yeah, yeah.
So they end up, like, I guess it really comes down to, like, if you're working with a CPA that just isn't experienced in real estate, that's what, like, they just don't, like, catch these things.

So I think, and this is just kind of like across the board, the biggest thing that I see, no matter if it's C-Corps, S-Corps, or partnerships, is the lack of knowledge that small business owners have for retirement.
They don't even have retirement plans.
And that's the biggest tax planning vehicle that we have.
And they don't even have good people to set up a retirement plan.
And so that, no matter what the industry is, that's like one of the most important things that small business owners just don't, they're not taking advantage of.
Why do you think that is?
I think they just don't know.
Because a lot of times people are coming from a W-2 job, right?
And so they're offered a 401k and that's all that they know, right?
And there are just so many different types of plans out there.
And, you know, I mean, people have brokers that do like their taxable accounts, but they don't, or they have someone who will manage their IRA.
There's just so much more to the world than an IRA and a 401k.

And it's pretty hard to kind of dig through what's even on the internet trying to, well, what's the difference between a simple IRA and a SEP and what are the events, you know, because some you can have and you don't have to fund for like small employees.
Some you have to, and you have to match what you're going to put in.
And so it's like, it's like this giant mystic world of retirement that's, it's like hard to scratch the surface.
And I think because small business owners are so busy running a business, advertising, doing all the things, it's like a second thought.
